Summary: Anti-PD-1 antibodies have shown promise in treating tumors including metastatic melanoma. In melanoma, tumor-derived exosomal PD-L1 binds PD-1 on PD-1+ CD8 T cells and inactivates those T cells. Exosomal PD-L1 shares the membrane topology of cell-surface PD-L1 with its extracellular domain exposed on exosome surfaces and binds PD-1 in a concentration-dependent manner. Interferon-γ increases PD-L1 on extracellular vesicles and increases secretion of exosomal PD-L1. Interferon-γ treatment increases exosome binding to PD-1. Exosomal PD-L1–mediated inhibition systemically impairs anti-tumor immunity and enables tumor cells to counteract immune pressure at the effector stage. Anti-PD-1 and anti-PD-L1 antibodies disrupt PD-L1 binding to PD-1 and block exosomal PD-L1–mediated T cell inhibition. In patients with metastatic melanoma, circulating exosomal PD-L1 levels positively correlate with interferon-γ and change during anti-PD-1 therapy, and an early on-treatment increase in circulating exosomal PD-L1 stratifies clinical responders from non-responders to anti-PD-1 therapy.
References: Chen, G., Huang, A. C., Zhang, W., Zhang, G., Wu, M., Xu, W., Yu, Z., Yang, J., Wang, B., Sun, H., Xia, H., Man, Q., Zhong, W., Antelo, L. F., Wu, B., Xiong, X., Liu, X., Guan, L., Li, T., … Guo, W. (2018). Exosomal PD-L1 contributes to immunosuppression and is associated with anti-PD-1 response. Nature, 560(7718), 382-386. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-018-0392-8
